title: Thought-Action-Observation Pattern
concept: The standard prompt pattern: generating a Thought about state, then an Action tool call, then receiving an Observation.

exercises.prompt: In the ReAct approach, why does mixing reasoning with actions beat just planning or just acting?

options.text: It lets the agent think, act, then adjust based on what really happened
It makes the agent respond faster by skipping all thinking
It removes the need for any tools at all

exercises.explain: Interleaving reasoning and acting wins because the agent can plan a step, try it, and then use the real result to fix its next move. Pure planning ignores reality, and pure acting has no thoughtful guidance.

cards.title: A Repeating Beat
cards.body: ReAct agents follow a steady rhythm: think, do, look. That loop repeats until the job is done.

cards.title: Thought → Action → Observation
cards.body: A Thought is the agent reasoning about the current situation. An Action is a tool it calls. An Observation is the result the tool sends back.

cards.title: Exploring a Dark Room
cards.body: You think 'the switch is by the door' (Thought), you reach for it (Action), and you feel the wall (Observation). Then you think again and reach again.

cards.title: Weather Question
cards.body: Thought: 'I need today's weather.' Action: call the weather tool for London. Observation: 'It is 14°C and rainy.' Now the agent can answer.

cards.title: Not Just One Round
cards.body: Myth: the pattern happens only once. Truth: Thought-Action-Observation repeats in a loop, each cycle building on the last observation.

cards.title: Inside vs Outside
cards.body: Thoughts and Actions come FROM the agent. Observations come TO the agent from the outside world.

left.text: Thought and Action are written by the agent itself.

right.text: Observation is real feedback the tool gives back.

exercises.prompt: Which item in the pattern is the result that a tool sends BACK to the agent?
exercises.explain: The Observation is the feedback returned from a tool after the Action runs. The agent reads it to decide its next Thought.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In the ReAct pattern, the Action step is where the agent calls a tool.
exercises.explain: Correct! The Action is exactly the moment the agent uses a tool, like searching or calling an API, to take a concrete step.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing step of the pattern.
exercises.sentence: First the agent writes a Thought, then it takes an Action, and finally it reads the Observation.
exercises.explain: After the Action runs, the tool returns an Observation. The agent reads that result before thinking again.

exercises.prompt: Which of these looks like an AI agent following the Thought-Action-Observation pattern?

options.text: Thought: I should check the price. Action: call price_lookup('shoes'). Observation: $49. Thought: Now I can answer.
Just tell me the price of the shoes right now please.
The shoes are on sale this weekend at the mall downtown.

exercises.explain: The first one is AI-generated agent reasoning: it clearly shows a Thought, then an Action tool call, then an Observation with the result, then the next Thought.

exercises.prompt: An agent needs to find how many days until a user's flight. It writes 'I need today's date and the flight date,' calls a calendar tool, and gets back 'Flight: June 10; Today: June 3.' What are the three parts here, in order?
exercises.explain: The agent first reasons about what it needs (Thought), then calls the calendar tool (Action), then receives the dates back (Observation). This think-do-look order is the core rhythm of every ReAct step, and it repeats until the answer is ready.
